chunks.text: Graph Representations Prof. Timothy Sun Graphs A graph G consists of vertices V and edges E. Graphs A graph G consists of vertices V and edges E. Graphs Edges can be: ● directed ● weighted ● non-simple Graphs Edges can be: ● directed ● weighted ● non-simple Edges capture pairwise relationships. Graph representations Two fundamental operations: ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” (Edge list) Store all edges in a list: (Edge list) Store all edges in a list: ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” (Edge list) Store all edges in a list: ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” O(|E|) ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” Θ(|E|) Store all edge information in a matrix: Adjacency matrix Adjacency matrix Store all edge information in a matrix. ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” Adjacency matrix Store all edge information in a matrix. ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” Θ(1) ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” Θ(|V|) Adjacency list Each vertex has a list of outgoing edges/neighbors: Adjacency list Each vertex has a list of outgoing edges/neighbors: ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” Adjacency list Each vertex has a list of outgoing edges/neighbors: ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” d(v) is the degree of v. Adjacency list Each vertex has a list of outgoing edges/neighbors: ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” O(min(d(u), d(v))) ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” Θ(d(v)) d(v) is the degree of v. Each vertex has a list of outgoing edges/neighbors: ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” O(min(d(u), d(v))) ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” Θ(d(v)) d(v) is the degree of v. (for directed graphs, out/indegree) Adjacency list Each vertex has a (hash)map of outgoing edges: Adjacency map Adjacency map Each vertex has a (hash)map of outgoing edges: ● “Is there an edge between u and v?” Θ(1)-ish... ● “What are all the edges leaving v?” Θ(d(v)) Which is the best? Adjacency lists seem better when graphs are sparse. Which is the best? Adjacency lists seem better when graphs are sparse. Adjacency matrices seem better when graphs are dense. Which is the best? Don’t adjacency maps have the best of both worlds? List Matrix Map is_edge(u,v) min(d(u), d(v)) 1 1 (“expected”) neighbors(v) d(v) |V| d(v) Which is the best? ● Matrices better on very dense graphs. Which is the best? ● Matrices better on very dense graphs. ● Many algorithms (e.g. DFS) don’t use the edge query operation. Which is the best? ● Matrices better on very dense graphs. ● Many algorithms (e.g. DFS) don’t use the edge query operation. ● Graphs are sometimes implicit. Which is the best? ● Matrices better on very dense graphs. ● Many algorithms (e.g. DFS) don’t use the edge query operation. ● Graphs are sometimes implicit. We’ll only use adjacency lists from here on. Connectivity Is this graph connected? Connectivity Is this graph connected? No, there are two “islands” Connectivity Is this graph connected? No, there are two “islands” One island is not “reachable” from the other. Connectivity A graph is connected if you can walk from any vertex to any other vertex. A (connected) component of an undirected graph is a maximal connected subgraph. Reachability A walk starts at one vertex and moves along a sequence of edges to another vertex. Reachability A walk starts at one vertex and moves along a sequence of edges to another vertex. A path is a walk with no repetitions. Given a speciﬁc vertex v, which vertices are reachable from v by a walk/path? Reachability Given a speciﬁc vertex v, which vertices are reachable from v by a walk/path? “Something-ﬁrst” search Proof sketch: induct on BFS layers “Something-ﬁrst” search “Something-ﬁrst” search What’s the runtime? “Something-ﬁrst” search What’s the runtime? ● Depends on the bag “Something-ﬁrst” search What’s the runtime? ● Depends on the bag: if insert and remove take O(T) time, then total runtime is O(E’T), where E’ is the number of edges in v’s component “Something-ﬁrst” search What’s the runtime? ● Depends on the bag: if insert and remove take O(T) time, then total runtime is O(E’T), where E’ is the number of edges in v’s component “Something-ﬁrst” search When “bag” is a stack or queue, T = O(1). Connected components Try doing a “something-ﬁrst” search on every vertex. Try doing a “something-ﬁrst” search on every vertex. Connected components Try doing a “something-ﬁrst” search on every vertex. Connected components Runtime: O(|V| + |E|) Connected components Runtime: O(|V| + |E|) ← linear time Connected components Can extend slightly to record component labels Connected components Connected components Can extend slightly to record component labels “Fill” tool in Paint “Fill” tool in Paint “Fill” tool in Paint The ﬁll rule: ● Color orthogonal neighbors if they have the same color. ● Repeat until no more pixels remain. “Fill” tool in Paint “Pretend” like you have a graph. ● Vertices are pixels “Fill” tool in Paint “Pretend” like you have a graph. ● Vertices are pixels ● Edge if neighbors are same color “Fill” tool in Paint “Pretend” like you have a graph. ● Vertices are pixels ● Edge if neighbors are same color The component of the selected pixel is colored. Strong connectivity u and v are strongly connected if u is reachable from v, and vice versa. Strong connectivity u and v are strongly connected if u is reachable from v, and vice versa. A graph G is strongly connected if every pair of vertices is strongly connected. Strong connectivity not strongly connected strongly connected Strong connectivity How can we check for strong connectivity? Strong connectivity How can we check for strong connectivity? First attempt: do a DFS at every vertex and check if every vertex is reached Strong connectivity How can we check

and vice versa. Strong connectivity u and v are strongly connected if u is reachable from v, and vice versa. A graph G is strongly connected if every pair of vertices is strongly connected. Strong connectivity not strongly connected strongly connected Strong connectivity How can we check for strong connectivity? Strong connectivity How can we check for strong connectivity? First attempt: do a DFS at every vertex and check if every vertex is reached Strong connectivity How can we check for strong connectivity? First attempt: do a DFS at every vertex and check if every vertex is reached O(|V|(|V|+|E|)) runtime Strongly connected components Strong connectivity is an equivalence relation on the vertices Strongly connected components Strong connectivity is an equivalence relation on the vertices ● Reﬂexive: v ~ v ● Symmetric: u ~ v → v ~ u ● Transitive: u ~ v and v ~ w → u ~ w Strongly connected components Equivalence relations induce a partition into strongly connected components. Strongly connected components Second attempt: 1. Pick any v 2. DFS(v) on forward edges 3. DFS(v) on backward edges and check if every vertex is reached Strongly connected components Second attempt: 1. Pick any v 2. DFS(v) on forward edges 3. DFS(v) on backward edges and check if every vertex is reached Runtime: O(|V|+|E|), but why does it work? Strongly connected components If the algorithm succeeds, every vertex belongs to the same SCC as v. Strongly connected components If the algorithm succeeds, every vertex belongs to the same SCC as v. There’s only one strongly connected component.
